footer_tagline_after_name: — a public-interest journalism project. Records, corrections, and tips welcome. Data is from public records and may contain errors or omissions.

rf_banner_hire_follow: We'll turn the noise into signal.
source_material_notice: Source data and documents (agency names, MOAs, official records) remain in English as originally published.
home_meta_description_with_count: {count} local law enforcement agencies have signed 287(g) agreements with ICE, authorizing officers to enforce federal immigration law. We tracked all of them.
home_meta_description_no_data: Tracking local law enforcement agencies with 287(g) agreements authorizing officers to enforce federal immigration law.
home_hero_lead: Under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, local police agencies can sign agreements with ICE that authorize their officers to perform immigration enforcement. The program has expanded dramatically since 2025.
home_stat_agencies_footnote: *Unique FBI identifiers; some identifiers have multiple agency names.
home_hero_state_callout_standard: In {state}, {agency_pct}% of agencies are participating, covering {pop_pct}% of the state population.
home_hero_state_callout_fl: In {state}, {agency_pct}% of agencies are participating, covering {pop_pct}% of the state population. Florida mandates 287(g) cooperation under SB 168 (2019).
home_hero_state_callout_none: No agencies in {state} currently participate in 287(g).
home_map_subhead: Click any dot to view the agency's agreement and records.
home_map_size_note: Dot size scales with sworn officer count (FBI LEE).
home_map_caveat: This map shows agencies currently participating in 287(g). For example, an agency that stopped its participation last month does not show on this map.
home_map_statewide_note: {count} statewide agencies aren't shown on the map.
home_map_boundaries_note: Dots mark where an agency is based, not the area it covers. Jurisdiction boundaries are in the works.
home_map_empty_subtitle: Run the pipeline to generate agency data.
home_models_subhead: The three 287(g) program models grant different scopes of authority to local officers.
model_jem_short: If officers suspect a person is illegally in the country and is detained, they can check databases or interview them about their citizenship.
model_jem_detail: Participating officers may issue civil detainers — requests for jails to hold people beyond their scheduled release so ICE can take custody. JEM operates inside detention facilities only, not in the community. It is the oldest and most common 287(g) model.
model_tfm_short: This is the broadest authorization. Officers work alongside ICE agents in the community to make immigration arrests.
model_tfm_detail: TFM grants the broadest authority of the three models. Participating officers can stop, question, and arrest individuals in the community — not only those already in custody. They operate jointly with ICE field agents on targeted enforcement operations.
model_wso_short: This is the most limited model. Officers execute ICE administrative warrants on specific named individuals already incarcerated or detained.
model_wso_detail: Introduced around 2020, the WSO model is narrower than JEM or TFM. Officers may only serve administrative warrants on specific individuals ICE has already identified for removal. They cannot initiate independent enforcement or conduct community operations.
home_search_baseline: {rows} rows across {agencies} agencies in {states} states & territories
about_meta_description: About this project and how to get involved.
about_intro_1_after_name: is a public interest journalism project documenting local law enforcement agencies that have partnered with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act.
about_intro_2: The 287(g) program allows ICE to delegate immigration enforcement authority to state and local agencies through a formal Memorandum of Agreement. Officers at participating agencies can be trained and authorized to perform immigration enforcement functions — including identifying, processing, and detaining people for removal.
about_why_1: 287(g) Watch builds on an ecosystem of excellent work tracking ICE and 287(g) agreements. Our goal is to make this data easy for people to access and act on, to be visible to search bots and AI, and to produce powerful visuals. We will continue to enrich the tool with historical data, contact information, and key context from sources on the ground.
about_involve_lead: Know something about an agency? Have records? See an error? We want to hear from you.
about_involve_records: File public records requests to surface local data
methodology_meta_description: Data sources, limitations, and methods used in this project.
methodology_sources_lead: Agency data is compiled from the following sources:
methodology_source_ice_body: — the current official list of participating agencies published by ICE. Scraped and archived regularly.
methodology_source_appelson_body: — a community archiving effort that maintains historical ICE spreadsheets and individual MOA PDFs going back to 2016.
methodology_source_lee_body: — jurisdiction population, sworn officer and civilian counts, and staffing ratios (officers per 1,000 residents). Each row carries its own data year; we keep the most recent.
methodology_source_wikidata_body: — agency website URLs sourced via SPARQL queries against the Wikidata knowledge graph.
methodology_source_muckrock_body: — linked FOIA requests for individual agencies, sourced from MuckRock's public request database.
methodology_source_census_body: — county, place, and county-subdivision centroid coordinates used to position agencies on the map.
methodology_source_geocodio_body: — a geocoding service used to locate the handful of agencies (university and airport police, regional authorities) the Census files don't recognize by name.
methodology_matching_body: Agency names vary across sources. We normalize names and use fuzzy string matching to link records across datasets, then hand-review ambiguous matches. Each agency in our index includes a confidence score for its cross-source matches.
methodology_mapping_body: The source data gives each agency's state and usually its county, but not a precise address. We place each agency at a representative point: municipal police are mapped to the center of their town or city, and county sheriffs and other county-level agencies to the center of their county, using U.S. Census Bureau gazetteer coordinates. A handful of agencies the Census files don't recognize — university and airport police, a few regional authorities — are located with the Geocod.io geocoding service. These are approximate points showing where an agency is based, not the area it operates in; jurisdiction boundaries are in the works.
methodology_mapping_statewide: Statewide agencies — state police, departments of corrections, and similar bodies — are not shown on the map, because a single dot would misrepresent a jurisdiction that covers the whole state. They remain in the data and in all counts.
methodology_population_heading: A note about jurisdiction and population
methodology_population_body_1: Population jurisdiction has always been a fraught but useful metric in understanding the activities of law enforcement agencies. We include it here to provide a very rough sense of the scale of law enforcement involved in 287(g) agreements, while taking care not to overcount overlapping jurisdictions (e.g. the population of a city is contained by the population of the county).
methodology_population_body_2: Population data is from the FBI's Law Enforcement Employment dataset and reflects each agency's jurisdictional residential population — the residents of the area the agency serves, not necessarily the people encountering immigration enforcement. And jurisdiction size and enforcement scope diverge sharply by agency type and program model: a state highway patrol's jurisdiction is the whole state but its work looks nothing like a small-town police department's, and a county sheriff running a Jail Enforcement Model only touches people who get booked into the jail.
methodology_limitations_li_1: Some agencies have had agreements lapse and rejoin. Historical participation dates may be incomplete.
methodology_limitations_li_2: MOA PDFs are not available for all agencies. We link to the appelson archive where available.
methodology_updates_body: The pipeline is re-run regularly to capture new ICE announcements. The data snapshot date is shown on each page.
methodology_license_body: Data compiled by this project is made available under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license. If you use this data, please attribute: Recovered Factory / 287(g) Watch (287g.recoveredfactory.net).
attribution_body: 287(g) Watch is a project of Recovered Factory, built by Tory Lysik and David Eads.
glossary_meta_description: Definitions of key terms used in 287(g) immigration enforcement reporting.
glossary_subtitle: Key terms used in reporting on the 287(g) program and immigration enforcement.
agency_meta_description_base: {agency_name} in {state} has a 287(g) agreement with ICE.
agency_history_intro: Changes recorded since tracking began. Gaps between entries mean no changes were detected.
agency_source_moa: As reported in the agency's MOA
agency_moa_body: The full agreement between {agency_name} and ICE is available below.
agency_contact_none: Contact information for this agency is not yet available.
agency_records_intro: The 287(g) participation data doesn't tell us anything about what happened after the agreement was signed. To learn more, the public will need to ask or formally request information. MuckRock is organizing public records requests about immigration enforcement nationwide:
agency_records_matched_intro: A public records request has already been filed on this agency:
agency_records_guide_label: How to follow the paper trail of ICE's local immigration enforcement
agency_records_diy_cta: Sign up with MuckRock to file your own request
home_map_use_cta: Download our free videos and images →
usemap_meta_title: Use our 287(g) map — free to share | 287(g) Watch
usemap_meta_description: Download the 287(g) Watch map of every active 287(g) agreement as a video, GIF, or static image and use it for free under a Creative Commons license.
usemap_intro: Our map of 287(g) agreements is free for you to use and share as a video, GIF, or static image. Just credit us.
usemap_video_label: Animated map of active 287(g) agreements growing from January 2025 to the present
usemap_download_note: The MP4 is best for video editors and social posts; the GIF is best for quick embeds and messaging.
usemap_aspect_note: This is presented in the natural aspect ratio of the content — it's up to you to frame it in your videos however you like.
usemap_license_body_suffix: (Attribution). You're free to share, adapt, and reuse it, including commercially, as long as you credit 287(g) Watch — ideally with a link back to 287g.recoveredfactory.net.
usemap_license_ask: Don't alter the figures. The numbers come straight from our dataset, and changing them would misrepresent the data.
usemap_custom_body_prefix: Want a map of just your state or region, or a white-labeled version?
